message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:p>
                  Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2 (SARS-COV-2), previously known as 2019-nCoV, is responsible for the atypical pneumonia pandemic designated as Coronavirus Disease 2019 (COVID-19). The number of cases continues to grow exponentially reaching 492,000 people in 175 countries as of March 25, 2020. 22,169 people (∼4.5%) infected with SARS-COV-2 virus have died. We have developed an exponential regression model using the COVID-19 case data (Jan 22 – Mar 22, 2020). Our primary model uses designated
                  <jats:italic>Phase 1</jats:italic>
                  countries, who exceed 2500 cases on Mar 22. The model is then applied to
                  <jats:italic>Phase 2 countries</jats:italic>
                  : those that escaped the initial
                  <jats:italic>Phase 1</jats:italic>
                  global expansion of COVID-19. With the exception of
                  <jats:italic>stabilizing countries</jats:italic>
                  (South Korea, Japan, and Iran) all
                  <jats:italic>Phase 1 countries</jats:italic>
                  are growing exponentially, as per
                  <jats:italic>I</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sub>2500</jats:sub>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  ) = 120.4 ×
                  <jats:italic>e</jats:italic>
                  <jats:sup>
                    0.238
                    <jats:italic>t</jats:italic>
                  </jats:sup>
                  , with a rate,
                  <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  = 0.238 ± 0.068. Excluding China, the BRICS developing nations and Australia are in
                  <jats:italic>Phase 2</jats:italic>
                  . Case data from
                  <jats:italic>Phase 2 countries</jats:italic>
                  are following the model derived from
                  <jats:italic>Phase 1 countries</jats:italic>
                  . In the absence of measures employed to flatten the curve including social distancing, quarantine, and healthcare expansion, our model projects over 274,000 cases and 12,300 deaths in the US by Mar 31. India can expect 123,000 cases by April 16. By flattening the curve to the growth rate of
                  <jats:italic>stabilizing countries</jats:italic>
                  (
                  <jats:italic>r</jats:italic>
                  = 0.044 ± 0.062), the US would prevent 8,500 deaths by Mar 31, and India would prevent 5,500 deaths by April 16.
                </jats:p>
title: A deductive approach to modeling the spread of COVID-19
